title: Analytical and Compressed Simulation of Noisy Stabilizer Circuits
authors: Paul Aigner, Jasmin Matti, Maria Flors Mor-Ruiz, Julius Wallnöfer, Wolfgang Dür
abstract: We develop analytical and algorithmic techniques that enable efficient simulation of a broad class of noisy stabilizer circuits. We derive closed-form expressions of expectation values for tensor product of Paulis in circuits with non-deterministic Pauli measurements, yielding an efficient strong simulation method that avoids explicit density matrix construction and enables direct noise parameter sweeps. We introduce a circuit compression framework that reduces the per-sample cost of weak simulation in general noisy stabilizer circuits, including deterministic measurements, by separating parameter-independent preprocessing from sampling. Finally, we extend the analytical framework beyond its standard domain to include a small number of deterministic measurements, general rotations, and non-diagonal noise channels. Our results provide a unified framework for both strong and weak simulation of noisy stabilizer circuits and corresponds to an extension of the noisy stabilizer formalism introduced in \cite{PhysRevA.107.032424}. They offer applications ranging from calculation of the expectation values of entanglement witnesses, determination of reduced states, to energy evaluation.
summary: This paper presents a new framework for the efficient simulation of noisy stabilizer circuits. It provides an analytical method to compute exact Pauli expectation values, allowing for efficient noise parameter sweeps without statistical error. Additionally, it introduces a circuit compression technique that speeds up sampling-based simulations by pre-processing the circuit. The framework is versatile enough to handle general rotations and non-diagonal noise, though complexity increases with deterministic measurements.
main_problem: The challenge of efficiently simulating large-scale noisy quantum circuits where the exponential overhead of density matrix propagation makes brute-force methods intractable.
main_result: The development of a unified framework for both strong (analytical) and weak (sampling-based) simulation of noisy stabilizer circuits, including an efficient circuit compression technique.
method: The authors use an extension of the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism (NSF) to derive closed-form expressions for Pauli expectation values and introduce a compression algorithm that separates parameter-independent preprocessing from sampling.
model_or_system: Noisy stabilizer circuits consisting of Clifford gates, Pauli-diagonal noise channels, and both deterministic and non-deterministic Pauli measurements.
key_observables: Pauli expectation values, entanglement witnesses, reduced density matrices, Bell-inequality witnesses, and energy expectation values for Hamiltonians with polynomial Pauli terms.
important_parameters: Noise strengths (lambda), number of qubits (n), number of Clifford gates, number of noise channels, and the number of deterministic measurements (which controls the branching complexity).
main_assumptions: The primary analytical results focus on Pauli-diagonal noise; the efficiency of the simulation decreases exponentially with the number of deterministic measurements and non-diagonal noise channels.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the three simulation regimes; Figure 2 illustrates the NSF update rules for gates and measurements; Figure 3 shows the Heisenberg action of noise; Figure 6 depicts the circuit compression methodology.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of noisy simulation, defines the Noisy Stabilator Formalism (NSF) and its analytical (strong) simulation capabilities, presents a circuit compression framework for efficient sampling (weak simulation), extends the framework to more complex noise and measurements, and provides complexity analysis and optimization techniques.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides powerful tools to study the impact of complex, correlated noise on quantum information protocols and entanglement properties without the cost of full density matrix simulation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Overview schematic illustrating the formalism’s capa- bilities: noisy stabilizer circuits can be efficiently processed to compute arbitrary Pauli expectation values or to gener- ate compressed circuit representations, enabling more efficient sampling.

Fig. 2. Overview of the NSF. (a) Illustrates the framework: an initial stabilizer state is processed by a noisy stabilizer circuit composed of non-deterministic measurements, Clifford gates, and Pauli-diagonal noise channels. The NSF maps this circuit to an equivalent representation with an updated initial stabilizer state, where the Clifford operations and measure- ments are absorbed into the state, and the noise channels are updated while preserving their Pauli Kraus rank. (b) Shows the update rule for Clifford gates: each Pauli noise term Nj is transformed by conjugation with the Clifford as the gate is pushed through the noise channel.
